attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman is admitted to hospital at 38 weeks gestation for induction of labour due to gestational diabetes. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her pulse rate is 80/min, blood pressure 120/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. A cardiotocograph (CTG) is commenced to monitor fetal well-being during the induction process.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes normal fetal heart rate variability for this patient on a cardiotocograph?
attributes.explanation:
In this scenario, understanding normal fetal heart rate variability on a cardiotocograph (CTG) is crucial for assessing fetal well-being during labour induction. 5-25 beats per minute is the correct range for normal fetal heart rate variability on a CTG.

0-3 beats per minute represents reduced variability, which can be a sign of fetal compromise. This pattern may indicate fetal sleep cycles, central nervous system depression (e.g., due to hypoxia or certain medications), or fetal anomalies. Persistent reduced variability requires further assessment and potentially intervention.

3-5 beats per minute also represents reduced variability and would be considered abnormal, requiring further assessment and potential intervention.

25-50 beats per minute would be considered increased variability. While not necessarily abnormal, it may be associated with fetal movement, maternal fever, or certain medications. It's important to interpret this in the context of other CTG features and clinical factors.

50-100 beats per minute is not used to describe fetal heart rate variability. This range is more relevant when discussing baseline fetal heart rate or accelerations/decelerations.

Variability refers to the beat-to-beat changes in fetal heart rate. It's an important indicator of fetal well-being as it reflects the interplay between the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems. Normal variability suggests an intact and functioning fetal central nervous system and good oxygenation status.

In addition to variability, other important features of CTG interpretation include baseline fetal heart rate (normally 110-160 beats per minute), the presence of accelerations, and the absence of concerning decelerations. All these factors should be considered together, along with the clinical context, when assessing fetal well-being during labour.
learningPoints: Normal fetal heart rate variability on a cardiotocograph (CTG) is 5-25 beats per minute.
Variability is an important indicator of fetal well-being and central nervous system integrity.
Reduced variability may indicate fetal compromise and requires further assessment.
references: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Intrapartum fetal surveillance clinical guideline – Fourth edition. East Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2019. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Intrapartum-Fetal-Surveillance.pdf
